sn35.247:1.1: “Suppose that a man, wounded and festering, were to go into a swampy jungle.
sn35.247:1.2: Its sharp-bladed grasses would pierce his feet; its thorns would scratch his festering sores.
sn35.247:1.3: And so, from that cause, he would experience an even greater measure of pain and unhappiness.
sn35.247:1.4: In the same way, there is the case where a certain monk, having gone to a village or to the wilderness, meets up with someone who upbraids him:
sn35.247:1.5: ‘This venerable one, acting in this way, undertaking practices in this way, is a thorn of impurity in this village.’
sn35.247:1.6: Knowing this person to be a thorn, one should understand restraint and lack of restraint.
sn35.247:2.2: There is the case where a monk, seeing a form with the eye, is set on pleasing forms, is repelled by unpleasing forms, and remains with body-mindfulness unestablished, with limited awareness.
sn35.247:2.3: He doesn’t discern, as it has come to be, the awareness-release, the discernment-release, where any evil, unskillful mental qualities that have arisen utterly cease without remainder.
sn35.247:2.7: “Touching a tactile sensation with the body…
sn35.247:2.8: “Cognizing an idea with the intellect, he is set on pleasing ideas, is repelled by unpleasing ideas, and remains with body-mindfulness unestablished, with limited awareness.
sn35.247:2.9: He doesn’t discern, as it has come to be, the awareness-release, the discernment-release, where any evil, unskillful mental qualities that have arisen utterly cease without remainder.“
sn35.247:3.1: Just as if a person, catching six animals of different ranges, of different habitats, were to bind them with a strong rope.
sn35.247:3.2: Catching a snake, he would bind it with a strong rope.
sn35.247:3.7: a monkey, he would bind it with a strong rope.
sn35.247:3.8: Binding them all with a strong rope, and tying a knot in the middle, he would set chase to them.
sn35.247:3.9: “Then those six animals, of different ranges, of different habitats, would each pull toward its own range & habitat.
sn35.247:3.10: The snake would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the anthill.’ The crocodile would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the water.’ The bird would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll fly up into the air.’ The dog would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the village.’ The hyena would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the charnel ground.’
sn35.247:3.11: The monkey would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the forest.’ And when these six animals became internally exhausted, they would submit, they would surrender, they would come under the sway of whichever among them was the strongest.
sn35.247:3.12: In the same way, in any monk whose mindfulness immersed in the body is undeveloped & unpursued, the eye pulls toward pleasing forms, while unpleasing forms are repellent. The ear pulls toward pleasing sounds… The nose pulls toward pleasing aromas… The tongue pulls toward pleasing flavors… The body pulls toward pleasing tactile sensations…
sn35.247:3.13: The intellect pulls toward pleasing ideas, while unpleasing ideas are repellent.
sn35.247:3.14: This, monks, is lack of restraint.
sn35.247:4.2: There is the case where a monk, seeing a form with the eye, is not set on pleasing forms, is not repelled by unpleasing forms, and remains with body-mindfulness established, with immeasurable awareness.
sn35.247:4.3: He discerns, as it has come to be, the awareness-release, the discernment-release, where all evil, unskillful mental qualities that have arisen utterly cease without remainder. “Hearing a sound with the ear… “Smelling an aroma with the nose…
sn35.247:4.4: “Tasting a flavor with the tongue… “Touching a tactile sensation with the body…
sn35.247:4.5: “Cognizing an idea with the intellect, he is not set on pleasing ideas, is not repelled by unpleasing ideas, and remains with body-mindfulness established, with immeasurable awareness.
sn35.247:4.6: He discerns, as it has come to be, the awareness-release, the discernment-release, where all evil, unskillful mental qualities that have arisen utterly cease without remainder.“
sn35.247:5.1: Just as if a person, catching six animals of different ranges, of different habitats, were to bind them with a strong rope.
sn35.247:5.2: Catching a snake, he would bind it with a strong rope.
sn35.247:5.7: a monkey, he would bind it with a strong rope.
sn35.247:5.8: Binding them all with a strong rope, he would tether them to a strong post or stake.
sn35.247:5.9: “Then those six animals, of different ranges, of different habitats, would each pull toward its own range & habitat.
sn35.247:5.10: The snake would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the anthill.’ The crocodile would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the water.’ The bird would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll fly up into the air.’ The dog would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the village.’ The hyena would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the charnel ground.’ The monkey would pull, thinking, ‘I’ll go into the forest.’
sn35.247:5.11: And when these six animals became internally exhausted, they would stand, sit, or lie down right there next to the post or stake.
sn35.247:5.12: In the same way, in any monk whose mindfulness immersed in the body is developed & pursued, the eye doesn’t pull toward pleasing forms, and unpleasing forms are not repellent. The ear doesn’t pull toward pleasing sounds… The nose doesn’t pull toward pleasing aromas… The tongue doesn’t pull toward pleasing flavors… The body doesn’t pull toward pleasing tactile sensations…
sn35.247:5.13: The intellect doesn’t pull toward pleasing ideas, and unpleasing ideas are not repellent.
sn35.247:6.1: “The ‘strong post or stake’ is a synonym for mindfulness immersed in the body.
sn35.247:6.3: ‘We will develop mindfulness immersed in the body. We will pursue it, give it a means of transport, give it a grounding. We will steady it, consolidate it, and set about it properly.’
sn35.247:6.4: That’s how you should train yourselves.”
